nativeRange.habitat: Submerged or semi-submerged in slow-moving or still freshwater; forms dense mats on bedrock, cobbles, root masses and submerged wood. Most commonly reported in the eastern United States (Midwest, South, Northeast) with sparse populations in western states and across temperate and Mediterranean regions of Europe, Mexico, Chile, Australia, Zambia and the Arabian Peninsula.

decorPreferences: driftwood (especially branchy or upright pieces that showcase the fan-shaped fronds)
porous rock, lava rock, seiryu, or other textured stones
stainless or plastic mesh sheets for moss walls and carpets
tied with cotton thread / fishing line or attached with cyanoacrylate gel until rhizoids anchor
never buried in substrate — the base will rot if covered
gentle to moderate flow keeps the iconic fan-shaped fronds free of detritus

goodWith: tetras, rasboras and other small community fish
biotope tanks for North American or Mediterranean streams

summary: Iconic dense fan-shaped aquarium moss with a distinctive overlapping leaf-pocket structure. Generally advised to attach to wood, rock, or mesh rather than bury in substrate; tolerates a wide range of light and water conditions and is one of the few mosses with a confirmed North American native distribution.
careNotes: Fissidens fontanus is one of the most visually distinctive aquarium mosses, forming compact fan-shaped fronds of overlapping 'pocket' leaves (2-7 mm long with a conspicuous midrib) rather than the loose, stringy growth typical of Java Moss or Christmas Moss. It is a true aquatic bryophyte and is unusual among hobby mosses for being native to North America (especially the eastern United States) in addition to occurrences across Europe, the Mediterranean, Mexico, Chile, Australia and Zambia. ATTACHMENT: This is an epiphyte and should be tied or glued to driftwood, porous rock, lava rock, or plastic/stainless mesh; do NOT bury it in substrate, as the base will rot if covered. Over several weeks rhizoids anchor the moss to the surface, after which thread or fishing line can be removed. LIGHTING is tolerant of low to medium levels; the moss survives in low-light setups, but moderate to higher light (PAR roughly 20-60 micromoles at the moss surface) produces denser, more compact fan-shaped growth. CO2 is optional — the plant thrives without it, though supplementation (around 20-30 ppm) noticeably increases growth rate and frond density. GROWTH RATE is slow across all sources, which is generally considered an advantage for detailed aquascaping where the iconic fan shape is the focus. SIZE is typically 2-5 cm in height per frond. TEMPERATURE tolerance is roughly 20-26 °C (68-79 °F); some sources extend the range to 18-28 °C, but stability matters more than the exact value. pH 6.0-7.5; soft to moderately hard water. FERTILIZATION demand is low — the moss absorbs nutrients from the water column and does not require a nutrient-rich substrate; some sources note it can react poorly to heavy chemical dosing. SHRIMP AND FRY REFUGE: dense Fissidens mats are excellent microhabitat for newly hatched fry and for dwarf-shrimp larvae, which graze biofilm and microfauna from the fronds. PARAMETER STABILITY is important — sudden swings can cause patches of melting or rotting. SOURCING NOTE: the originally-assigned primary Buce Plant search URL (buceplant.com/products/fissidens-fontanus) returned only navigation chrome at fetch time and was abandoned per the playbook's primary-failure rule; the correct Buce Plant product page (buceplant.com/products/fissidens-fontanus-1) was used as the manifest primary, with Fish Laboratory and Wikipedia as cross-verifying secondaries. Source spread on temperature was modest (Fish Laboratory: 18-25 °C; aggregate hobby sources: 20-28 °C; Buce-Plant clone: 'around 25 °C'), averaged to 20-26 °C. Source spread on pH was modest (Fish Laboratory: 5.0-7.5; Wikipedia and Buce Plant: 6.0-8.0; aggregate hobby sources: 6.0-7.5), settled on 6.0-7.5.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation only under aquarium conditions: cut or pull off any healthy fragment of the fan and re-attach it to a new surface — every piece regrows. There is no need to wait for a reproductive cycle. True sexual reproduction (spore production from a sporophyte) requires emersed growth and is essentially never observed in fully submerged aquarium tanks.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for: care level (easy/beginner), lighting (medium), CO2 (not necessary; injection improves growth and coloration), pH 6.0-8.0, growth rate (slow), placement (attach to rocks/driftwood; readily adheres without frequent re-tying), substrate (standard aquarium substrate works; quality soil enhances growth), max size 3-5 cm height, native range (United States), parameter-stability requirement (tolerates variable conditions but avoids drastic changes). Originally-assigned URL (buceplant.com/products/fissidens-fontanus) returned navigation chrome only; this trailing-'-1' product page is the live Fissidens Fontanus listing on Buce Plant.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Phoenix Moss (Fissidens Fontanus): Ultimate Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verification of high-stakes fields. Used for: lighting (low), CO2 (not required, beneficial), temperature 18-25 °C / 65-77 °F (narrower than the aggregate range), pH 5.0-7.5 (wider on the acid side), slow growth rate, placement (closer to the center of the tank to showcase fountain shape; can be used as backdrop or carpet), propagation by cutting one plant into fragments that re-attach to wood/stone/plastic mesh within days, native range (North America), care difficulty (not a difficult plant to keep). Confirmed the slow growth rate and the do-not-fertilize-aggressively caveat.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Fissidens fontanus
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Fissidentaceae, order Dicranales, class Bryopsida, division Bryophyta), authoritative scientific name and authority (Fissidens fontanus (Bach.Pyl.) Steud.), common names (Limp Pocket Moss, Phoenix Moss), full native-range distribution (eastern United States plus Europe, Mediterranean, Mexico, Chile, Australia, Zambia, Arabian Peninsula), habitat (slow-moving or still water; mats on flat bedrock, cobbles, root masses or logs), and morphology (lance-like 2-7 mm leaves with a conspicuous midrib, characteristic pocket-like structures at leaf bases). Wikipedia is taxonomy-only per the playbook source whitelist, but it is the most reliable confirmation of the family and native range for this species.
